content:
The patient presented to the emergency department with acute onset substernal chest pain and dyspnea. Initial electrocardiogram demonstrated ST-segment depression in the anterior leads with reciprocal changes. Troponin I was elevated at 2.8 ng/mL, and subsequent serial measurements peaked at 4.1 ng/mL, consistent with acute myocardial infarction. The patient was immediately initiated on dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and ticagrelor, along with unfractionated heparin. Urgent cardiac catheterization revealed significant stenosis of the left anterior descending coronary artery with TIMI II flow. Given the demand-related nature of the infarction and the patient's hemodynamic stability, percutaneous coronary intervention was performed with successful placement of a drug-eluting stent to the LAD with restoration of TIMI III flow. Post-procedure angiography demonstrated excellent stent apposition with no residual stenosis.

During the hospital course, the patient remained hemodynamically stable with no recurrent chest pain or arrhythmias. Transthoracic echocardiography on hospital day two revealed mild hypokinesis of the anterior wall with preserved overall left ventricular ejection fraction of 48 percent. B-type natriuretic peptide was mildly elevated at 287 pg/mL. The patient was transitioned to oral beta-blocker and ACE inhibitor therapy, which were titrated to target doses. High-intensity statin therapy was initiated, and the patient demonstrated good tolerance to all medications without adverse effects.

The patient was discharged on hospital day three in stable condition with appropriate follow-up arranged. Discharge medications include aspirin, ticagrelor, metoprolol, lisinopril, and atorvastatin. The patient was counseled on medication adherence, cardiac risk factor modification, and the importance of outpatient cardiology follow-up within two weeks for repeat echocardiography and functional assessment.
